title: Welcome to FoundryPress
summary: Your FoundryPress system is live. Here’s what it does and how to start using it.
meta_description: Your FoundryPress system is live. Learn how the structure works and how to start building content with it.
body:
Welcome to FoundryPress.

If you're reading this, your publishing system is live — and more importantly, it's working exactly as it should.

But FoundryPress isn’t just about getting an article to display. It’s about giving you a clean, structured way to build and manage content without the weight of traditional platforms.

Most systems start simple and become complicated over time. Plugins stack up. Performance slows down. Structure gets messy. And eventually, it becomes harder to maintain than it is to build.

FoundryPress was designed to solve that.

Instead of layering complexity, it starts with a simple foundation:

  • A central Hub to manage your system
  • Structured brand folders for organization
  • JSON-based content for clarity and control
  • Clean routing for predictable, readable URLs

Everything has a place. Everything is understandable. Everything is yours.

From here, you can begin creating real content, building out your brands, and developing a system that grows with you instead of working against you.

This isn’t just a demo.

It’s the starting point for a better publishing workflow.

Take a few minutes to explore the Hub, review the structure, and create your first real article.

Once you do, you’ll understand how everything connects.

And more importantly — how simple it can be.
author: FoundryPress
og_description: A clean, structured publishing system built for clarity, control, and scalability.
hook: You now have a working publishing engine.
excerpt: Your FoundryPress system is live and ready.

title: Why I Built FoundryPress (And Why I Stopped Using WordPress)
summary: A simple explanation of why FoundryPress was built and the problems it was designed to solve.
meta_description: Why FoundryPress was created, what problems it solves, and how it offers a simpler way to build and manage content sites.
body:
I didn’t set out to build a publishing system.

I was just trying to solve a problem.

Like a lot of people, I started with WordPress. It worked. It was flexible. And in the beginning, it felt like the right tool.

But over time, things changed.

Every new idea required another plugin. Every update risked breaking something. Performance became harder to manage. And the structure — the thing that should make everything easier — started to feel messy and unpredictable.

It wasn’t one big problem. It was a hundred small ones.

And eventually, I realized I wasn’t building anymore. I was maintaining a system that kept getting heavier.

So I stepped back and asked a simple question:

What would this look like if it were simple from the start?

That question became FoundryPress.

Instead of building on layers of abstraction, FoundryPress is built on a clear structure:

  • A Hub to manage everything in one place
  • Brands organized in predictable folders
  • Articles stored as structured JSON data
  • A clean rendering layer for the frontend

No database. No plugin dependencies. No hidden complexity.

Everything is visible. Everything is understandable. Everything is under your control.

That doesn’t mean it does everything.

It means it does the right things — and does them well.

FoundryPress isn’t trying to replace every tool. It’s designed for a specific kind of builder:

  • Someone who wants clarity instead of complexity
  • Someone who prefers structure over shortcuts
  • Someone who is building systems, not just pages

If that’s you, this will make sense quickly.

If it’s not, that’s okay too.

But if you’ve ever felt like your tools were getting in the way of your work…

This is a different approach.

And once you start using it, it’s hard to go back.
author: Thomas Kloos
og_description: A simpler approach to building and managing content systems.
audience: creators, developers, and builders who want structured content systems
hook: What would a publishing system look like if it were simple from the start?
excerpt: A simpler way to build content systems without the weight of traditional platforms.
